channel.title: Rhinoceros — Lansing Public Records Investigations
channel.description: Public records investigations into local government, campaign finance, and corporate influence in Michigan.

item.title: Lansing: Fact-Checking the March 23 Deep Green Hearing
item.description: What Deep Green, the mayor, and Bloom Energy said at the March 23 public hearing, checked against the public record. The tax revenue doesn't go where they said. The fire funding isn't in the budget. And Bloom's VP told Council fuel cells generate no toxic chemicals. The EPA says otherwise.

item.title: Lansing: The Garza Conflict, Spelled Out
item.description: Jeremy Garza is VP of the union that gets the contracts. The Charter says officers with conflicts cannot vote. The land sale needs his vote to pass. Here is why the conflict exists and what residents can do about it.

item.title: Lansing Council: Donor Cheat Sheet
item.description: A public records lookup of campaign contributions from LRC-PAC, UA Local 333, and other influential donors to each of the eight sitting Lansing City Council members.

item.title: Lansing: A Conflict Question Could Reshape the Deep Green Land Sale Vote
item.description: The Lansing City Charter requires six of eight votes to sell city property and bars members with financial interests from voting. Council Member Garza is VP of UA Local 333, which stands to benefit from the Deep Green project. If he cannot vote, the sale cannot pass.

item.title: Can Michigan Communities Trust the Data Center Coalition's "Independent" Information?
item.description: Michigan for Responsible Data Centers says it provides neutral information to communities evaluating data center proposals. Public records show its members fund the politicians who approve those projects, through channels designed to hide the source of the money.

item.title: Michigan's New Data Center "Information" Coalition Has the Hallmarks of an Astroturf Campaign
item.description: Michigan for Responsible Data Centers launched March 19 promising 'clear, accurate information.' Its website is managed by a PR firm that represents a data center developer. Its cited economic analysis was paid for by a coalition member. Its university guidebook was reviewed by coalition members before publication. No individuals are named anywhere on the site.

item.title: Lansing: Is the Council Being Set Up to Vote on Deep Green Blind? The Public Record Says Yes.
item.description: The council votes soon on the Deep Green land sale and rezoning. Public records raise questions about undisclosed contracts, TIFA tax capture, missing studies, and an NDA that may not be legally defensible.

item.title: Lansing: Public Utility, Private Contract: The Deep Green Fuel Cell Deal
item.description: Deep Green would buy the fuel cells. BWL would own them. Bloom would operate them. The contracts are under NDA. Bloom's fuel cells in Delaware lasted seven years. The proposed BWL contract is twenty.

item.title: Deep Green's Projected $933K in Tax Revenue Does Not Reach the General Fund
item.description: LEAP projects $933,081 in annual property tax from the Deep Green data center. Michigan TIF law diverts roughly half to the TIFA before it reaches any taxing authority. The mayor was corrected on camera.

item.title: The Lansing Chamber Funds Campaigns, Runs Advocacy, and Reports $0 in Political Spending
item.description: The Lansing Regional Chamber's IRS filing shows $311,344 in fees with no vendor names and $0 in political spending. It operates a PAC that funded 6 of 8 Council members and endorsed a US Senate candidate but does not appear in the FEC database or the IRS Form 8871 disclosure system.

item.title: How Organic Is the Support for Deep Green?
item.description: Seven of the 12 template support letters generated through a paid advocacy platform for the Deep Green data center came from Lansing Regional Chamber members, award recipients, or policy committee participants. The Chamber published four press releases supporting the project without disclosing any of these connections.

item.title: Bloom Energy: Fined, Sued, and Subsidized in Three States
item.description: Bloom Energy will operate the Deep Green data center's fuel cells in Lansing. In Delaware, Bloom received $580M+ in subsidies, was fined $1.37M by the EPA, and had fuel cells decommissioned after seven years.

item.title: Lansing: Did BWL's Board Know About Deep Green When It Approved a $100M Steam Conversion?
item.description: The Lansing Board of Water and Light approved a $100M steam-to-hot-water conversion without mentioning Deep Green, the data center project whose waste heat would fill the resulting gap. The same consultant validated both sides.

item.title: Lansing Is Michigan's Fastest-Rising Rental Market
item.description: Zillow, HUD, and Census data confirm Lansing rents rose 25.3% since 2022, outpacing every Michigan metro. Three ZIP codes saw double-digit annual increases, hitting the lowest-income neighborhoods hardest.

item.title: Lansing: BWL Steam Item Listed on Park Board Agenda, Missing from Minutes
item.description: The Lansing Park Board's December 2025 agenda listed five items including BWL Wentworth Park Steam Conversion. The minutes listed three. The missing item sits on BWL's Deep Green construction corridor. The November meeting where it was introduced has zero documents on file.

item.title: Who Owns Lansing? 24 Landlords Control 946 Rental Parcels
item.description: An analysis of 42,175 Lansing tax parcels reveals 24 mega-landlords controlling 946 rental properties, with eviction filing rates exceeding 100% for six of them. 10.4% of rental parcels are owned out of state.

item.title: Lansing Council Member Garza: Union VP Who Votes on Union Projects
item.description: Jeremy Garza is VP of UA Local 333 and a Lansing City Council member. His entire $48,050 campaign came from unions and PACs. He has never recused from a vote.

item.title: Lansing Chamber PAC Funded 6 of 8 Council Members
item.description: The Lansing Regional Chamber's LRC-PAC contributed $30,750 to 6 of 8 sitting Council members. Its endorsed candidates win at rates above 85%. The same executives run both the PAC and project advocacy.

item.title: Deep Green Support Letters Were Platform-Generated
item.description: All 12 support letters submitted to Lansing City Council for the Deep Green data center were routed through The Soft Edge, an advocacy platform. Email header forensics reveal template clusters and loopback addresses.

item.title: Deep Green PR Rep Was a Lansing Planning Commissioner
item.description: Josh Hovey served on the Lansing Planning Commission through June 2025. Five months later he lobbied the same body as Deep Green's paid PR representative. He sits on the PAC that funded 6 of 8 Council members.

item.title: How Consumers Energy's Dark Money Reaches Lansing City Hall
